cognates.note: True cognate: Skt. √jñā and the Greek/Latin/Germanic ‘know’-words all descend from PIE *ǵneh₃-. English ‘gnosis’ is a learned Greek borrowing, not an independent reflex, but it shares the same root.

invariant_definition: *Jñāna* is cognition or knowing, but the term carries more structure than the English word 'knowledge' usually does. It belongs to the full epistemic apparatus of *pramāṇa*, *pramātṛ*, *prameya*, *pramā*, and *apramā*, and it ranges from ordinary perceptual episodes to liberating realization. Different schools analyze its ontological status differently: as a quality of the self, as a mental modification illuminated by consciousness, as the soul's expansive substantive attribute, or as a momentary cognition-stream. Vedāntic usage further differentiates *jñāna* from *vidyā*, *anubhava*, *sākṣātkāra*, and *aparokṣa-jñāna*: ordinary cognition may be valid yet non-liberating, while liberating cognition is the precise point at which ignorance is removed or God becomes directly present. The term therefore presupposes broader disputes about *vṛtti*, witness-consciousness, selfhood, and the relation between knowing and being. In practice, when a text says that liberation requires *jñāna*, the real question is always: what sort of cognition, of what object, produced by what means, and with what metaphysical consequence?

school_framing.shared_core: *Jñāna* means **cognition / knowledge as a determinate epistemic state with veridical force**, distinguished from mere *vṛtti*, *smṛti*, or *śravaṇa*. All Vedāntic and Brāhmaṇical schools treat *jñāna* as either (a) cognitive episodes whose *pramāṇa* is shared, or (b) the liberating cognition of the highest object (Brahman, Bhagavān, *tattva*, or *puruṣa-prakṛti-viveka*). Schools differ on whether liberating *jñāna* alone suffices (*jñāna-niṣṭhā*, Advaita), or requires *bhakti*/*upāsanā*/*karma* as co-operative means.
school_framing.register_axes_note: Schools differ on register (E vs. S vs. AD) and on whether *jñāna* alone is *mokṣa-sādhana*. Real disagreement (AF1) between Advaita and Mīmāṃsā / Tattva-vāda on *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* and on whether *jñāna* without *bhakti* is sufficient. Buddhist *jñāna* (*prajñā*) names cognition of *śūnyatā*/*tathatā* — same structural role, opposed content.

per_school.definition: In Advaita, liberating *jñāna* is the śāstra-generated ascertainment of *ātmatattva* through the *mahāvākya*, since those Vedāntic sentences alone are *pramāṇa* for the sentence-compatible meanings of *tat* and *tvam* [[SB Upodghāta](cite://madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/upodghāta)]. Avidyā is beginningless *anirvācyājñāna*, and *mokṣa* consists in its cessation through *jñāna* alone rather than through any *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* [[SB 1](cite://madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/1.prathamo vibhāgaḥ.1)] [[UpS I.17.7-9](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9)]. Accordingly the Veda is the operative *pramāṇa* for the rise of brahma-jñāna, whose grasp of sentence-meaning removes the *anyadṛṣṭi* that is avidyā [[UpS I.17.7-9](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.7-9)] [[NS 2.9-10](cite://sureshvara/naishkarmya-siddhi/2.9-10)].
primary_loci: Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, Siddhānta-Bindu, Prathamo Vibhāgaḥ, prose under verse 1

per_school.definition: Viśiṣṭādvaita treats *jñāna* of the true nature of *jīva* and Paramātman as the indispensable antecedent to *dhyāna*, *arcana*, and *upāsanā*, not as a self-sufficient cancellation of the world [[§3](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§3)] [[§4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)]. The Vedāntic sentences are said to make known the self as *dehātirikta*, its own *svarūpa*, the *antaryāmin* who is its inner ruler, and the unsurpassed brahmānubhava that follows upon worship [[§4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)]. The *jīva*'s own *svarūpa* is described as *jñānānandaika-guṇa* and, once karmically imposed differentiations are removed, as self-manifest and expressible only as *jñāna-svarūpa* [[§5](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§5)].

In Tattva-vāda, *jñāna* is necessary for *mokṣa* but never autonomous, since liberation does not arise without Hari and Viṣṇu alone is *jñānada* to the ignorant and *mokṣada* to the knower [[Anuvy. 1.1.13-15](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.13-15)]. The jīva's knowing remains irreducibly relational because the *bhedapañcaka* is real and beginningless, so the cognition relevant to release confirms rather than sublates difference [[Anuvy. 1.4.111-112](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.4.111-112)]. Later scholastic formulation accordingly defends the intrinsic graspability of a cognition's validity within a realist *prāmāṇya* theory [[TT 1, Prāmāṇyavāda, svato-grāhyatvānumāna](cite://vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/1.prāmāṇyavāda)].

In Acintya-Bhedābheda, *jñāna* is not denied, but *uttamā-bhakti* is defined precisely as Kṛṣṇānuśīlana not overlaid by *jñāna-karmādi*, so knowledge-discipline is subordinated wherever it obstructs favorable devotion [[BRS 1.1.11](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.11)]. Jīva Gosvāmin accordingly reads the *abheda-śāstras* through Bhagavān's *acintya-śakti*, holding the aggregate of *jīvas* to stand to him in a ray-like relation of difference and non-difference [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Once the difference between Bhagavān and the *māyā-mohita* jīva is fixed, *bhajana* alone becomes the operative *abhidheya* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44)].

For Dvaitādvaita, the jīva is *jñāna-svarūpa*, atomic, body-distinct, and yet *harer adhīna*, so cognitive being itself manifests simultaneous dependence and distinctness [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)]. Bondage and liberation do not alter that ontological profile: the self is known as associated with beginningless *māyā* in saṃsāra, while devotional *upāsanā* removes the continuing darkness of *ajñāna* [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2)] [[DaŚ 6](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/6)]. Because Brahman is the self of all things, the school can describe all cognition as in this sense *yathārthaka*, while preserving the triadic articulation of Brahman, *cit*, and *acit* [[DaŚ 7](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/7)].

In Śuddhādvaita, *brahmajñāna* removes *avidyā* and accomplishes the soul's *svarūpa-yogyatā* for the attainment of Puruṣottama, but that attainment is not yet complete at the mere rise of knowledge [[BSB 1.1.12-19a](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.12-19a)]. Vallabha states that once Bhagavān chooses the *jīva* as his own, *bhakti-bhāva* supplies the auxiliary fitness by which Puruṣottama-attainment actually occurs, so *jñāna* is necessary but not self-sufficient within the school's grace-structured soteriology [[BSB 1.1.12-19b](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.12-19b)] [[BSB 1.1.12-19c](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.12-19c)]. This sequence presupposes the real status of the *jīva* as Brahman's own *aṃśa*, not an illusory appearance external to Brahman [[BSB 2.3.43-45a](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/2.3.43-45a)] [[BSB 2.3.43-45d](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/2.3.43-45d)].

In Sāṅkhya, *jñāna* is first located on the side of *buddhi*: Īśvarakṛṣṇa lists it among the *sāttvika* forms of the internal organ rather than as a changing property of *puruṣa* [[SK 23](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/23)]. Bondage is traced to *viparyaya*, while *apavarga* comes only through the discriminative cognition generated by *tattvābhyāsa*, a purified and non-errant knowledge that separates the self from the tattva-complex [[SK 44](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/44)] [[SK 64](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/64)]. The school's operative sense of *jñāna* is therefore *viveka* or *guṇa-puruṣa*-discrimination, not ritual performance or devotional apprehension [[SK 64](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/64)].

In Yoga, *jñāna* is first organized within the economy of *citta-vṛtti*: *pramāṇa*, *viparyaya*, and *vikalpa* are all cognitive modes, and error is explicitly defined as *mithyājñāna* [[YS 1.6-9](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.6-9)]. The practical sequence of *śraddhā-vīrya-smṛti-samādhi-prajñā* then ripens into a *prajñā* that is *ṛtaṃbharā* and distinct from *śruta* and *anumāna* because it takes a *viśeṣārtha* as its object [[YS 1.20](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.20)] [[YS 1.48-49](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.48-49)]. At the soteriological limit, the limbs of yoga produce *jñānadīpti* up to *vivekakhyāti*, and that uninterrupted discrimination is the direct means of hāna and *kaivalya* [[YS 2.26-28](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.26-28)].

In Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, *jñāna* is a real cognition of the self, designated interchangeably as *buddhi*, *upalabdhi*, and *jñāna*, and later synopsis divides it into *smṛti* and *anubhava*, with valid cognition treated as *yathārthānubhava* [[TSaṅg 34-36](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/34-36)]. Its episodic production depends on the appropriate contact-complex: Kaṇāda makes the presence and absence of cognition a *liṅga* of *manas*, while Annambhaṭṭa analyzes perception as *indriyārthasannikarṣajanya jñāna* of *nirvikalpaka* and *savikalpaka* kinds [[VS 3.2.1-3](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/3.2.1-3)] [[TSaṅg 42-43](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/42-43)] [[VS 8.3-5](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/8.3-5)]. Soteriologically, Nyāya treats bondage as rooted in *mithyājñāna*, above all the *anātmany ātmagrahaḥ*, and makes *tattvajñāna* the direct *upāya* to *apavarga* by cancelling that very object-directed delusion [[NyBh 4.2.1](cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/4.2.1)] [[TSaṅg 64-65](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/64-65)].

For Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, *jñāna* is governed by *pramāṇa*-specific *arthabodha*: Jaimini defines *dharma* through *codanā*, and Śabara immediately explains that injunction alone makes known a supersensible object inaccessible to the senses [[MS 1.1.2](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.2)] [[ŚBh on MS 1.1.2](cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.2-codanajanya-jñāna)]. The same discussion defines perception as the *buddhijanma* produced when sense and object are in contact, thereby situating ordinary cognition within a general epistemology from which Vedic *śabda* is then distinguished [[ŚBh on MS 1.1.4](cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.4-pratyaksa-buddhijanma)]. Śabara next argues that, because of the *autpattika* relation between word and meaning, Vedic verbal cognition of dharma is *pramāṇa* and not subject to the instability of merely human utterance [[ŚBh on MS 1.1.5](cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.5-sabda-pramana-jñāna)].

In Mādhyamaka, liberative knowing is inseparable from the discipline of the two truths: without reliance on *lokasaṃvṛti* the *paramārtha* is not taught, and without arrival at *paramārtha* nirvāṇa is not attained [[MMK 24.8](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.8)] [[MMK 24.10](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.10)]. That *paramārtha* is marked as *nirvikalpa* and *anānārtha*, while dependent arising itself is designated as *śūnyatā* and the *madhyamā pratipad* [[MMK 18.9](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.9)] [[MMK 24.18](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.18)]. Accordingly *jñāna* appears in a strictly soteriological register, as in the knowledge of the *pratyekabuddha* arising through non-association [[MMK 18.12](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.12)].

For Yogācāra, the manifold attribution of self and *dharma*s unfolds only within *vijñāna-pariṇāma*, whose threefold transformation structures the entire cognitive field [[Triṃ 1](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/1)] [[Triṃ 2](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/2)]. Conceptual construction is itself this transformed cognition, and what it projects as grasper-grasped duality lacks external objecthood; hence all this is *vijñapti-mātraka* [[Triṃ 17](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/17)] [[Triṃ 21](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/21)]. Liberative realization occurs only when cognition becomes established in *vijñapti-mātratā*, for until then the latent trace of dual grasping does not cease [[Triṃ 26](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/26)] [[Triṃ 27](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/27)].

In Pratyabhijñā-Trika, *jñāna* and *kriyā* are the very marks of living subjectivity, so cognition is not an adventitious predicate added to an otherwise inert self [[ĪPK 1.1.4-5](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.1.4-5)]. Utpaladeva accordingly rejects both the view that knowledge is an inert item and the view that it belongs to some other substrate: if *jñāna* were insentient it could not disclose an object, while if it is genuinely luminous it is inseparable from *citi* itself [[ĪPK 1.2.7-8](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.2.7-8)]. The decisive refinement is that pure *prakāśa* must already be *vimarśa*; hence differentiated cognitions such as *jñāna*, *saṃkalpa*, and *adhyavasāya* are contracted manifestations of the same self-reflexive consciousness that recognition restores to explicitness [[ĪPK 1.5.11-13](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.5.11-13)] [[ĪPK 1.5.18-19](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.5.18-19)].

In Jainism, *jñāna* is constitutive of the *mokṣamārga* itself, since the path is defined through the triad of right vision, right knowledge, and right conduct [[TS 1.1](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.1)]. Umāsvāti classifies knowledge fivefold as *mati*, *śruta*, *avadhi*, *manaḥparyāya*, and *kevala*, with the first two counted as indirect and the remaining three as direct [[TS 1.9-12](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9-12)]. He then analyzes *mati-jñāna* through memory, recognition, thought, and determination, and places *śruta* downstream from *mati*, so cognition is structured as an ascending hierarchy culminating in *kevalajñāna* rather than a single undifferentiated state [[TS 1.13-14](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.13-14)] [[TS 1.20](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.20)].

textual_history.summary: *Jñāna* is the abstract noun (√jñā + ana) for 'knowing'. In the Ṛgveda the root is overwhelmingly verbal and participial — *veda*, *jānāti*, *prajānan*, *vidvān* — and the abstract *jñāna* as a fixed term is essentially absent; knowing is something gods and seers *do*, not yet a reified faculty or path. The Upaniṣads make knowing soteriological: the verb-forms of √jñā/√vid mark the difference between bondage and release ('knowing thus, he becomes the All'; 'the knower of Brahman attains the highest'), and *jñāna* / *vijñāna* begin to crystallise as the liberating cognition itself. The classical systems then technicalise it: Sāṅkhya locates *jñāna* among the *sāttvika* states of *buddhi* and identifies the liberating form with discriminative *viveka* (SK 23, 64); Yoga organises it within the modes of *citta* and culminates in *vivekakhyāti* (YS 2.26–28). The *Gītā* gives the term its most influential career, making *jñāna* a *yoga* — a path co-ordinate with *karma* and *bhakti* — distinguishing *jñāna* from its applied realisation *vijñāna* (7.2), and exalting the *jñānī*, the one of knowledge, as dearest of devotees (7.16–18). The arc from Veda to Gītā is real for the *root*; the reification of *jñāna* as the liberating cognition and as a named discipline is an Upaniṣadic-and-later development.

stages.sanskrit: tat savitur vareṇyam bhargo devasya dhīmahi | dhiyo yo naḥ pracodayāt
stages.rendering: May we hold that excellent radiance of the god Savitṛ — he who may impel our thoughts (dhiyaḥ).
stages.note: In the Saṃhitā the knowing-words are verbal (*veda*, *jānāti*, *vidvān*) and the noun for inner cognition is *dhī* / *manīṣā*, not the abstract *jñāna*. The Gāyatrī's prayer that the god 'impel our *dhiyaḥ*' is the paradigmatic Vedic statement of illumined thought — knowledge as something kindled from without, not a stored possession. This is the conceptual prehistory of *jñāna*: the term itself stabilises only later, but the value placed on revealed insight is Vedic. (Resonance, not lexical genealogy: the word *jñāna* does not stand here.)

The Upaniṣadic turn that *jñāna* later inherits: cognition is no longer merely a means to ritual ends but is identified with the absolute. The Aitareya's *mahāvākya* uses the kindred form *prajñāna*; the same equation underwrites the Upaniṣadic refrain that liberation comes by *knowing* — e.g. the Muṇḍaka's 'the knower of Brahman becomes Brahman' (*brahmavid brahmaiva bhavati*) — where the verbal/abstract of √jñā/√vid carries the soteriological weight. This is the seedbed of the Vedāntic thesis, central to Advaita, that liberating *jñāna* alone removes ignorance.

stages.sanskrit: yacched vāṅmanasī prājñas tad yacchej jñāna ātmani | jñānam ātmani mahati niyacchet tad yacchec chānta ātmani
stages.rendering: Let the discerning one (prājña) restrain speech and mind into the [self of] knowledge; let him restrain knowledge (jñāna) into the great self; let him restrain that into the tranquil self.
stages.note: Here the noun *jñāna* appears as a stage of the inner ascent — speech and mind are gathered into *jñāna*, *jñāna* into the 'great self' (*mahān ātmā*). The verse stands inside the Kaṭha's faculty-hierarchy (see *buddhi* and *manas*), and several readers identify this *jñāna*-self with *buddhi*. It shows the abstract noun already functioning Upaniṣadically as a level of cognition to be transcended toward the self — a more reflective use than the Saṃhitā's verbs.

stages.sanskrit: adhyavasāyo buddhir dharmo jñānaṃ virāga aiśvaryam | sāttvikam etad rūpaṃ tāmasam asmād viparyastam
stages.rendering: Buddhi is determination (adhyavasāya); virtue, knowledge (jñāna), dispassion, and lordship [are] its sāttvika form; the tāmasa form is the reverse of this.
stages.note: Sāṅkhya places *jñāna* not in the *puruṣa* but among the *sāttvika* dispositions of *buddhi* — knowledge is a state of the determining intellect, paired with virtue and dispassion. The liberating form is the discriminative knowledge that separates *puruṣa* from *prakṛti* (SK 64, *tattvābhyāsān … na asmi na me na aham*), i.e. *jñāna* as *viveka*. This system-internal localisation of *jñāna* in *buddhi* is exactly what the *Gītā* presupposes when it speaks of the *jñāna* gained by *sāṅkhya-buddhi* (cf. 2.39).

stages.sanskrit: jñānaṃ te 'haṃ sa-vijñānam idaṃ vakṣyāmy aśeṣataḥ | yaj jñātvā neha bhūyo 'nyaj jñātavyam avaśiṣyate
stages.rendering: I shall declare to you in full this knowledge (jñāna) together with its realisation (vijñāna), knowing which nothing further here remains to be known.
stages.note: The *Gītā*'s signature distinction: *jñāna* as the doctrinal knowing of the truth, *vijñāna* as its lived, direct realisation. Śaṅkara glosses *jñāna* as the knowledge of the divine reality (*īśvara-tattva-viṣayaṃ*) and *vijñāna* as making it one's own experience (*anubhava*). The verse marks *jñāna* as the totalising, terminal knowledge — 'knowing which, nothing else need be known' — the high Upaniṣadic valuation now framed as the Lord's own teaching.

stages.sanskrit: udārāḥ sarva evaite jñānī tv ātmaiva me matam | āsthitaḥ sa hi yuktātmā mām evānuttamāṃ gatim
stages.rendering: All these [worshippers] are noble, but the one of knowledge (jñānī) I hold to be my very self; for, integrated, he is fixed on Me alone as the highest goal.
stages.note: Of the four kinds who worship, the *jñānī* — the one possessed of *jñāna* — is singled out as 'my very self' (*ātmaiva me*) and 'dearest' (7.17). Here *jñāna* is no longer a state of *buddhi* (Sāṅkhya) but the mark of the supreme devotee: the *Gītā* fuses the knowledge-path with devotion, so that the highest *jñānī* is also the truest *bhakta*. This is the verse on which the whole later debate over *jñāna* versus *bhakti* (see the per-school material) turns.

translator_note: The lazy gloss 'knowledge' is usually too blunt. In modern English it suggests information, justified belief, or a stored content. *Jñāna* can mean a present cognitive event, an attribute of the self, a non-dual realization, or a devotional directness depending on school. 'Gnosis' sometimes helps, but the real safeguard is to specify the doctrinal setting rather than assume one English equivalent will do all the work.
